metadata.title: Spec Writing IS CS Education: What Changes When AI Codes
metadata.description_blurb: Geoffrey Challen's 2026 claim — 'the challenge is no longer turning specs into code; AI does that; the challenge is getting ideas out of students' brains and into specifications' — restructures what a CS class should assess.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B06: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: The assignment asked students to rewrite a vague prompt as a five-element specification. Claude could complete the original prompt in seconds. The specification took students thirty minutes. Some could not finish. They knew what they wanted. They could not get it out of their heads and into a form that a system could execute. Geoffrey Challen put it plainly in 2026: turning specs into code is AI's job now. Getting ideas into specs is still the student's. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, rewrite vague prompt as five-element specification

props.command: Rewrite this vague prompt as a five-element specification

beats.narration_text: Geoffrey Challen's 2026 claim reframes the discipline. The hard problem was always turning specifications into code — a skill machines now perform at scale. The problem that remains is getting ideas out of a student's brain and into a specification precise enough for a system to execute. That is a human skill. It requires problem framing, invariant naming, constraint articulation, and knowing what done looks like. AI does not make that easier. It makes it more visible.
beats.new_visual_element: Challen 2026 — 'turning specs into code: AI does that' / 'getting ideas into specs: humans' / visible not eliminated
beats.spark_line: Getting ideas into specs is still human.

beats.narration_text: The specification exercise requires two competencies that do not appear in syntax drills. Problem framing: naming the boundary of what the system should and should not do. Invariant judgment: deciding what must always be true regardless of input. Claude can code a sort function in seconds. It cannot decide whether Bell sorts before Adams on a GPA tie — because that is domain judgment, not a coding problem. The spec forces the student to supply that judgment explicitly.
beats.new_visual_element: Spec requires — problem framing / invariant judgment / not syntax / domain judgment is human

beats.narration_text: The build log rubric grades process, not output. Five dimensions: spec quality, handoff conditions, capacity labeling who does each step, artifact verification, and build log completeness. None require looking at the code. The code can be generated by Claude in a single prompt. The build log cannot — it requires decisions made at each step, documented as they happened. Grade the log, not the output.
beats.new_visual_element: Build log rubric — spec quality / handoff conditions / capacity labeling / artifact verify / log — none require reading code
beats.spark_line: Grade the log, not the code.

beats.narration_text: The classroom move is concrete: spec review before any code runs. Students submit their specification. Peers review it: is the operation clear? Are the invariants named? Is there a negative constraint? Is the done condition checkable? The review happens before Claude sees the project. What the peer review surfaces — vague invariants, missing constraints, unbounded output — is exactly what the code would have gotten wrong.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — spec review before code / peer checks invariants / vague spec = future bug / checkable done condition

beats.narration_text: Run the spec review exercise before any code this week. Paste this.

props.command: Review this specification before any code runs. Check: (1) Is the operation named precisely — testable verb, named inputs and output type? (2) Are the invariants stated — conditions that must always hold? (3) Is there a negative constraint — what the system must not do? (4) Is the done condition checkable by someone who did not write the spec? Flag any element that is vague. Return a revised specification with each gap addressed.

beats.narration_text: If this makes one spec precise before the code runs, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeTitleOutro — Spec Writing IS CS Education.
